walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Ethnos set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. You will see a map divided into six colored territories. Each territory has a name and a few spaces for glory tokens. This is where the area-control portion of the game happens.

walkthrough.title: Place Glory Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the glory tokens. These are the round tokens with point values on them. Each territory on the board has a spot for a stack of glory tokens. Place the appropriate glory tokens face up on each territory as shown in the rulebook. The top token is what gets awarded first, so territories become less valuable as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Choose Six Tribes
walkthrough.text: Now here is one of the coolest parts of Ethnos. The game comes with twelve different tribe types, but you only use six per game. Choose six tribe types to include. For your first game, the rulebook recommends a specific set, but after that feel free to mix and match. The six tribes you pick will dramatically change how the game feels each time you play.

walkthrough.title: Build the Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the cards for your six chosen tribes and shuffle them together into one big deck. Each tribe has cards in all six territory colors, so you will have a deck full of cards that combine tribe and color. Then shuffle the three dragon cards into the deck as well. The dragon cards are what trigger the end of each age.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal one card face down to each player. This is your starting hand. The rest of the deck stays face down in the center of the table as the draw pile. Leave space next to it for a face-up discard area where cards will accumulate during the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Control Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes all the control markers of that color. These are the small discs or tokens you will place on the board to claim territory presence. Keep them in a pile near you.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Score Track
walkthrough.text: Each player places one marker on the zero space of the score track around the edge of the board. This is where you will track your points across all three ages. Pick a starting player at random and you are ready to go.

bullets: Place board centrally — 6 colored territories visible
Each territory has spaces for glory tokens

summary.title: Place Glory Tokens
bullets: Stack glory tokens face up on each territory
Top token awarded first — territories lose value over time

summary.title: Choose Six Tribes
bullets: 12 tribe types available, pick 6 for this game
Use recommended set for first game
Tribe selection changes game feel dramatically

bullets: Shuffle all cards from the 6 chosen tribes together
Each tribe has cards in all 6 territory colors
Shuffle in the 3 dragon cards

bullets: Deal 1 card to each player
Rest of deck = face-down draw pile
Leave space for face-up discard area

summary.title: Hand Out Control Markers
bullets: Each player picks a color
Take all control markers of that color

summary.title: Set Up the Score Track
bullets: Place 1 marker per player on score track at 0

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Ethnos is played over three ages. Each age, you will recruit allies and play bands of cards to gain control of territories and earn points. At the end of each age, everyone scores for territory control and for the sizes of bands they have played. After three ages, the player with the highest total score wins.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Two Choices
walkthrough.text: You either recruit one ally by drawing a card, or you play a band of cards from your hand. That is it. Simple, right? But the decisions get tricky fast because of what happens after you play a band. We will get to that in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Recruiting an Ally
walkthrough.text: When you recruit, you draw exactly one card. You can take the top card of the face-down draw pile, or you can pick up any one card from the face-up discard area. The discard area will grow throughout the age as players discard cards, so keep an eye on what is available there. Sometimes the perfect card is just sitting in the discard waiting for you to grab it.

walkthrough.title: Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: You can never have more than ten cards in your hand at any time. If you already have ten cards, you cannot recruit. You must play a band instead. This is important because playing a band forces you to discard your entire remaining hand, so managing your hand size is a huge part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Band
walkthrough.text: A band is a set of cards from your hand that all share the same tribe or all share the same color. A band can be as small as one card or as large as six. You choose one card in the band to be the leader. The leader is special for two reasons: the leader's tribe ability activates, and the leader's color determines which territory you place a control marker in.

walkthrough.title: Band Examples
walkthrough.text: Let's say you have three Dwarf cards in red, blue, and green. Those three cards all share the Dwarf tribe, so they form a valid band. You pick one as the leader, say the blue Dwarf. The Dwarf tribe power activates, and you place a control marker in the blue territory. Alternatively, if you have a red Dwarf, a red Wizard, and a red Troll, those all share the red color and form a valid band of three.

walkthrough.title: Placing a Control Marker
walkthrough.text: After playing a band, you place one of your control markers on the territory that matches the leader's color. You only place a marker if your band has at least two cards. A single-card band activates the tribe power but does not place a control marker. Each territory can hold multiple markers from different players, and even multiple markers from the same player.

walkthrough.title: The Big Catch: Discarding Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Here is the painful part. After you play a band, you must discard all remaining cards in your hand face up to the discard area. Every single card you did not include in the band goes into the public discard pile where other players can pick them up. This is why timing your bands is so critical. Play too early and you waste potential. Wait too long and the age might end before you get to play.

walkthrough.title: Dragon Cards and Age End
walkthrough.text: The three dragon cards are shuffled into the deck. Whenever a dragon card is drawn, it is immediately placed face up and does not go into anyone's hand. When the third dragon card is revealed, the current age ends immediately. No more turns. This means the age can end suddenly, and any cards in your hand when it does are simply lost. You never know exactly when the age will end, so do not hoard cards too long.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Territory Control
walkthrough.text: At the end of each age, you score territories. For each of the six territories, check who has the most control markers there. The player with the most markers takes the top glory token from that territory. In case of a tie, nobody gets the token. Second place and beyond get nothing unless you are using the optional rules for higher player counts. After scoring, leave all markers on the board. They carry over to the next age.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Band Bonuses
walkthrough.text: You also score points based on the sizes of the bands you played during the age. A band of one or two cards scores zero bonus points. A band of three scores one point. A band of four scores three points. A band of five scores six points. And a band of six scores ten points. Bigger bands are dramatically more valuable, so try to build up large sets when you can.

walkthrough.title: Between Ages
walkthrough.text: After scoring, gather up all the played bands and discarded cards. Reshuffle everything, including the three dragon cards, into a new deck. Deal one card to each player again. The discard area resets to empty. Markers on the board stay where they are. This means your territory presence accumulates across ages, making early investment in a territory pay off later.

walkthrough.title: Tribe Powers
walkthrough.text: Each of the twelve tribes has a unique power that activates when that tribe's card is the leader of a band. Powers vary widely. Some let you place extra markers, some let you draw cards, some give bonus points, and some interact with the board in special ways. Since only six tribes are used per game, learn the six in play and think about how to leverage their abilities.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After three ages of scoring, the game is over. Add up your total points from all three ages, including territory glory tokens and band bonuses. The player with the highest total wins. In case of a tie, the tied player with the most control markers on the board wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 3 ages of recruiting allies and playing bands
Score territory control + band size bonuses each age
Highest total after 3 ages wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Realm
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first age with three example players. We will show you how recruiting, playing bands, and territory control all work together. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: Check Your Tribes
walkthrough.text: Before we start, take a moment to look at which six tribes are in the game. Read each tribe's power on the reference card or rulebook. A very common beginner mistake is not paying attention to which tribes are available. If you do not know what each tribe does, you might miss powerful combos or waste cards on tribes that are not even in the game.

walkthrough.title: The Opening Turns
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with just one card. The first few turns are always about recruiting. Player one draws from the deck. Player two draws from the deck. Player three draws from the deck. Nobody has enough cards to play a meaningful band yet, so everyone is building up their hand. This is normal and expected.

walkthrough.title: Building Toward a Band
walkthrough.text: After a few rounds of recruiting, Player one has five cards. Three of them are Halflings in different colors, and two are random other cards. Player one could play those three Halflings as a tribe band right now. But should they? Remember, playing a band means discarding those other two cards face up. Let's think about it.

walkthrough.title: The Hand Discard Trap
walkthrough.text: Player one decides to keep recruiting, hoping to build a bigger band. They draw two more cards, bringing their hand to seven. But here is the problem: those extra cards are not Halflings, and they do not match anything else useful. When Player one finally plays their three-Halfling band, they will discard four cards face up instead of two. That is four cards other players get to pick from. This is a classic beginner mistake: holding too many cards means losing more when you play.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play Bands Lean
walkthrough.text: If you have three matching cards and only one or two leftovers, play now. Do not wait and accumulate cards you will just have to throw away. Every discarded card is a gift to your opponents.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Plays a Band
walkthrough.text: Player two has collected four cards, three of which are the same color: green. They play a band of three green cards, choosing a Wizard as the leader. The Wizard's tribe power activates, and Player two places a control marker on the green territory. They discard their one remaining card face up. Clean play, minimal waste.

walkthrough.title: Scouting the Discard Area
walkthrough.text: Now here is something many new players overlook. Player two just discarded a card face up. Player three looks at the discard area and spots a red Troll that would complete a set they are building. Instead of drawing blind from the deck, Player three recruits the red Troll from the discard. The discard area is a goldmine. Always check it before drawing from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring the Discard
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Player one draws blindly from the deck again without even glancing at the discard area. They get a random card that does not help them at all. If they had looked at the discard, they would have seen a Halfling sitting right there. Do not make this mistake. The discard area is free information and often has exactly what you need.

walkthrough.title: The First Dragon
walkthrough.text: Player three draws from the deck and flips over a dragon card. The dragon goes face up immediately and does not go into anyone's hand. That is dragon number one. Two more dragons are somewhere in the deck. The age could end soon, or it could be a while. This uncertainty is what makes Ethnos exciting and tense.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring the Dragon
walkthrough.text: Player one sees the dragon and shrugs it off. They still have a big hand of seven cards and are trying to build an even bigger band. This is dangerous. If two more dragons come out quickly, the age ends and Player one loses all seven cards without playing anything. Dragons are a countdown timer. When you see one, start thinking about playing your best band soon rather than building forever.

walkthrough.title: Territory Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player two has been placing markers carefully. They now have two markers in the green territory and one in red. Player three also has one marker in green. Player two is thinking ahead. If they can play one more band with a green leader, they will have three markers in green and likely win that territory's glory token. This is how area control works: commit to a couple of territories and fight for majority.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Territories
walkthrough.text: Player one has been playing bands purely for size, chasing those big band bonuses. They have played two bands but spread their markers across four different territories, with just one marker in each. At scoring time, they probably will not have majority anywhere. Band bonuses alone are not enough to win. You need glory tokens from territories too.

walkthrough.title: The Age Ends Suddenly
walkthrough.text: The second dragon appears. Everyone tenses up. Two turns later, Player two draws the third dragon. The age ends immediately. Player one still has five cards in hand. All of them are lost. Player three had played their band last turn and had an empty hand. Player three lost nothing. This is why you cannot afford to hoard cards. The dragons will punish you.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the First Age
walkthrough.text: Now we score. First, territory control. Player two has the most markers in green, so they take the top glory token worth four points. Nobody else had majority anywhere without ties, so no other tokens are awarded this age. Next, band bonuses. Player two played a band of three for one point and a band of two for zero. Player three played a band of four for three points. Player one played two bands of three for one point each. Add it all up and move the score markers.

walkthrough.title: Reshuffle for Age Two
walkthrough.text: Gather all played bands and discards, shuffle everything back together with the three dragon cards, and deal one card to each player. The discard area is empty again. But notice: all the control markers stay on the board. Player two's lead in green territory carries over. In age two, Player three needs to place more green markers if they want to compete for that territory.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how recruiting, playing bands, and territory control all connect. You have watched players make common mistakes: hoarding cards too long, ignoring the discard area, forgetting about dragons, and neglecting territory presence. You are ready to play a full three-age game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and pacing advice.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all twelve tribes and key terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics and pacing advice
Reference the Appendix → tribe powers and key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Bands
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Ethnos is knowing when to play a band. New players tend to hoard cards, trying to build the perfect six-card band. But every extra card in your hand that is not part of the band becomes a gift to your opponents when you discard. Aim to play bands when you have minimal leftovers. A band of three with one discard is far better than a band of four with five discards. Play lean, play often.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Dragons
walkthrough.text: The three dragon cards are your clock. When the first dragon appears, take note. When the second one shows up, you should be in panic mode if you have a big hand. The third dragon ends the age immediately, and any cards in your hand are gone. A good rule of thumb: after the second dragon, play your best available band as soon as possible. Do not get caught holding a full hand when the age ends.

walkthrough.title: Focus Your Territory Presence
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to spread your control markers across all six territories. Resist that urge. You score glory tokens only for having the most markers in a territory. One marker in five territories usually scores nothing. Three markers in two territories can score you two glory tokens. Pick two or three territories and commit to them. Since markers persist across ages, early investment compounds over time.

walkthrough.title: Use the Discard Area
walkthrough.text: The face-up discard area is one of the most overlooked features for beginners. Every time someone plays a band, their leftover cards go face up where anyone can take them. Before you draw from the deck, always scan the discard. You might find the exact card that completes your set. Drawing blind from the deck when a perfect card is sitting in the discard is a wasted opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Tribes
walkthrough.text: Before each game, take thirty seconds to review the six tribes in play. Each tribe's power activates when it is the leader of a band. Some tribes are better for territory control, others for card draw, and others for bonus points. If you do not know the tribe powers, you will miss opportunities to maximize your bands. Also, ignore any tribe knowledge from previous games if those tribes are not in this game's set of six.

walkthrough.title: Balance Band Size and Territory
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to score in Ethnos: band bonuses and territory control. New players often focus too much on one and ignore the other. A player who plays many small bands for territory markers but never builds a big band misses out on the juicy six and ten point bonuses. A player who only chases big bands but scatters markers everywhere wins no glory tokens. The best players do both. They build focused bands that also place markers in their target territories.

summary.title: Timing Your Bands
bullets: Play bands when leftovers are minimal (1-2 discards)
Don't hoard cards trying to build the perfect band
Every discard is a gift to opponents — play lean, play often

summary.title: Watch the Dragons
bullets: 3 dragon cards = the age's countdown timer
After 2nd dragon → play your best band ASAP
Getting caught with a full hand when 3rd dragon hits = disaster

summary.title: Focus Your Territory Presence
bullets: Commit to 2-3 territories, not all 6
1 marker in 5 territories ≈ 0 points; 3 markers in 2 territories ≈ 2 glory tokens
Markers persist across ages — early investment compounds

summary.title: Use the Discard Area
bullets: Always scan the face-up discard before drawing from deck
Other players' leftovers may complete your sets
Drawing blind when a perfect card is in the discard = wasted turn

summary.title: Know Your Tribes
bullets: Review all 6 tribe powers before the game starts
Tribe powers activate when that tribe leads a band
Ignore tribes not in this game's set of 6

summary.title: Balance Band Size and Territory
bullets: Score from both band bonuses AND territory control
All small bands = no big bonus points; all big bands = no territory majority
Best players build focused bands targeting their chosen territories

walkthrough.title: Reading the Discard Economy
walkthrough.text: Advanced Ethnos is about information. Every time a player discards their hand, you can see exactly what they gave up. Pay attention to what colors and tribes they are discarding versus what they are keeping. If someone keeps discarding blue cards, they probably are not contesting the blue territory. If someone never discards Wizards, they are hoarding them. Use this information to decide which territories to contest and which cards to grab from the discard area.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: You can use your discards strategically. If you know an opponent needs red Trolls, avoid discarding red Trolls. Sometimes it is worth playing a slightly suboptimal band just to avoid feeding your opponent the exact card they need. Conversely, when you see an opponent play a band and dump useful cards, be ready to swoop in and recruit from the discard on your very next turn before someone else grabs them.

walkthrough.title: Dragon Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Experienced players track how deep they are in the deck relative to the three dragon cards. If you are halfway through the deck and no dragons have appeared, all three are in the bottom half and the age will likely run long. If two dragons appear early, the third is coming fast. Adjust your play accordingly. When dragons are bunched early, play smaller bands more frequently. When the deck is running long, you have time to build bigger bands.

walkthrough.title: Territory Investment Timing
walkthrough.text: Glory tokens decrease in value from age to age in each territory. The first token taken from a territory is worth the most. This creates an interesting dynamic: winning a territory in age one is worth more per token than winning it in age three. However, markers persist, so a strong presence built in age one can win you the territory in all three ages. The optimal play is to invest heavily in two territories during age one and defend that lead in later ages with minimal additional markers.

walkthrough.title: Tribe Synergy Combos
walkthrough.text: The real depth in Ethnos comes from combining tribe powers with your overall strategy. For example, tribes that let you draw extra cards pair well with aggressive territory strategies because you can rebuild your hand faster after playing bands. Tribes that give bonus points per marker pair well with a focused two-territory approach. Before the game starts, look at which six tribes are available and plan your strategy around the strongest synergies, not just individual tribe powers.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Age Territory Planning
walkthrough.text: Since markers stay on the board, think three ages ahead. If you place two markers in a territory in age one and win it, you start age two already ahead in that territory. Your opponents need to spend bands just to catch up while you can invest elsewhere or reinforce. The strongest positional play is to lock down two territories early and then use later ages to either reinforce those territories cheaply or pivot to grab a third territory where nobody else has committed.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Band Frequency
walkthrough.text: There is a tension between band quality and band frequency. Playing four bands of two or three cards gives you four control markers but low band bonuses. Playing two bands of five gives you massive bonuses but fewer markers. The optimal approach depends on the game state. In age one, prioritize markers to establish territory presence. In age three, when territory positions are largely set, shift toward bigger bands for bonus points. In age two, do a mix of both.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Tribe Strategy
walkthrough.text: Do not commit to a single tribe at the start of the game. Instead, let your draws guide you. If you keep getting Centaurs, build around Centaur bands even if you originally planned to focus on Wizards. The best players adapt to what the deck gives them rather than forcing a plan that the cards do not support. Be flexible with your tribe choices but rigid with your territory targets. Territories are the long game. Tribes are the tactical layer.

summary.title: Reading the Discard Economy
bullets: Track what opponents discard vs. keep
Frequent discards of a color = they're not contesting that territory
Use discard information to pick territories and target cards

summary.title: Controlling the Discard Pile
bullets: Avoid discarding cards your opponents need
Sometimes play a suboptimal band to deny key cards
Recruit quickly from discard after opponents dump useful cards

summary.title: Dragon Card Counting
bullets: Track deck depth vs. dragons revealed
0 dragons halfway through → age will run long, build big bands
2 dragons early → 3rd is coming fast, play small bands now

summary.title: Territory Investment Timing
bullets: Glory tokens decrease in value each age
Winning a territory in age 1 = most valuable token
Invest heavily in 2 territories early, defend cheaply later

summary.title: Tribe Synergy Combos
bullets: Card-draw tribes pair with aggressive territory plays (fast hand rebuild)
Bonus-point tribes pair with focused 2-territory approach
Plan around tribe synergies, not individual powers

summary.title: Multi-Age Territory Planning

Lock down 2 territories early, reinforce or expand later
Opponents must spend bands just to catch up

summary.title: Tempo and Band Frequency

Age 2: balance markers and band size
Age 3: shift toward big bands for bonus points

summary.title: Adaptive Tribe Strategy
bullets: Let your draws guide tribe focus — don't force a plan
Be flexible with tribes, rigid with territory targets
Tribes = tactical layer, territories = long game

entries.definition: A set of cards played from your hand that all share the same tribe or all share the same color. One card is designated as the leader. Bands can contain one to six cards.
The card you designate as the head of your band when you play it. The leader's tribe ability activates, and the leader's color determines which territory you place a control marker in.
One of the two actions you can take on your turn. Draw exactly one card, either from the top of the face-down draw pile or from the face-up discard area.

A disc or token in your player color placed on a territory when you play a band of two or more cards. The territory matches the leader's color. Multiple markers from the same player can occupy one territory.
One of six colored regions on the game board. At the end of each age, the player with the most control markers in a territory takes the top glory token. Ties award nothing.
A round token with a point value placed on each territory. The player with the most markers in a territory claims the top token. Tokens decrease in value as they are claimed across ages.
One of three rounds of play in Ethnos. Each age ends when the third dragon card is revealed. After scoring, all cards are reshuffled but control markers stay on the board.
One of three special cards shuffled into the deck. When drawn, a dragon is placed face up immediately and does not enter any player's hand. The third dragon ends the current age instantly.
The face-up pile of cards next to the draw deck. When a player plays a band, all remaining hand cards go here. Any player can recruit one card from the discard area instead of drawing from the deck.
The maximum hand size in Ethnos is ten cards. If you have ten cards, you cannot recruit and must play a band on your turn.
Points scored for the size of each band played during an age. Band of 1-2: 0 points. Band of 3: 1 point. Band of 4: 3 points. Band of 5: 6 points. Band of 6: 10 points.
Having the most control markers in a territory at the end of an age. The majority holder claims the top glory token. Ties result in no token being awarded.
Tribe. When a Centaur leads a band, you may immediately recruit a card from the deck or discard area without taking another turn. Useful for rebuilding your hand quickly after playing a band.
Tribe. When a Dwarf leads a band, the band contributes to a special Dwarf board track. Accumulating Dwarves across multiple bands earns escalating bonus points at end of age.
Tribe. When an Elf leads a band, you keep the band in front of you and it carries over to the next age instead of being shuffled back in. Powerful for maintaining progress.
Tribe. Giant bands score based on the total number of Giant bands you have played this age, not just the size of the current band. Rewards playing many small Giant bands.
Tribe. When a Halfling leads a band, you advance on the Halfling board track. The track provides escalating point bonuses the more Halfling-led bands you play across the age.
Tribe. When a Merfolk leads a band, you may place an additional control marker on a territory of your choice, regardless of the leader's color. Great for territory control.
Tribe. When a Minotaur leads a band, you may place your control marker in any territory, ignoring the leader's color restriction. Offers flexible territory placement.
Tribe. Orc bands score bonus points based on the number of Orc cards across all bands you have played this age. Rewards concentrating on Orcs throughout the age.
Tribe. When a Skeleton leads a band, you may add cards from the top of the draw pile to the band, potentially increasing its size and bonus points without using cards from your hand.
Tribe. Troll bands remain in front of you for future scoring. Each subsequent Troll band adds to your total, providing increasingly large band bonus points across the age.
Tribe. When a Wingfolk leads a band, the card counts as any color, allowing you to place your control marker on any territory. Provides maximum color flexibility.
Tribe. When a Wizard leads a band, you may immediately draw cards equal to the band size from the deck. Excellent for refilling your hand after playing a band.
